ontology.foodex2_description: Malt extract / malt syrup — processed cereal-derived sweetener
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for malt syrup as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for processed syrups is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of malt syrup is approximately 1 tablespoon (20 g), commonly used as a glaze, sweetener in baking, or flavouring agent in Chinese pastries and confections.

nutrient_source.title: Malt syrup — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for malt syrup (cr_id F07010003). All per-100g values are as reported. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for malt syrup (FDC ID 169652) for secondary validation; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Maltose GI is reported as approximately 105 (relative to glucose = 100) in the University of Sydney GI Database and in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Malt syrup is predominantly maltose; GI of 105 is applied directly. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (105 × 71.3 × 0.20) / 100 = (105 × 14.26) / 100 ≈ 15.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Extreme sugar load (71.3 g/100g) with zero dietary fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, compounding cardiovascular risk despite relatively low sodium content (35 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid malt syrup as a sweetener; its 71.3 g/100g sugar content promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and adiposity, both of which elevate blood pressure independently of dietary sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Malt syrup is predominantly maltose (GI ≈ 105, higher than glucose), with 71.3 g sugar/100g and zero dietary fibre, causing rapid and extreme postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Malt syrup is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its GI of approximately 105 and sugar content of 71.3 g/100g with no fibre buffer will cause severe postprandial hyperglycaemia and should be avoided entirely or restricted to trace culinary use only.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar intake (71.3 g/100g) with zero fibre promotes dyslipidaemia, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and elevated triglycerides, all of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should minimise malt syrup consumption; its concentrated free-sugar content (71.3 g/100g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and hepatic fat accumulation, increasing atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 71.3 g/100g with zero fibre promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no anti-inflammatory components are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid malt syrup; its high refined-sugar content (71.3 g/100g) promotes systemic inflammation and provides no anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malt syrup is a thick, dark brown, viscous liquid sweetener produced by malting barley grains, mashing them to convert starches into fermentable sugars via enzymatic action, and then concentrating the resulting liquid through evaporation. The primary sugar is maltose, a disaccharide with a glycemic index of approximately 105 — higher than glucose itself — making malt syrup one of the most glycaemically potent sweeteners available. Per 100 g, it provides 318 kcal, 71.3 g of sugar (entirely as maltose), 6.2 g of protein (residual barley proteins), negligible fat (0 g), and zero dietary fibre. Sodium is low at 35 mg/100g, and it contains modest amounts of potassium (320 mg), calcium (61 mg), and iron (0.96 mg). In Chinese culinary tradition, malt syrup (麦芽糖浆) is widely used as a glaze for roasted meats such as Cantonese roast duck and char siu, as a binding agent in confections, and as a sweetener in traditional pastries. It imparts a characteristic deep amber colour and a rich, malty sweetness. Clinically, malt syrup is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its extreme sugar density and high GI. It should be used only in trace amounts as a flavouring or glaze agent rather than as a primary sweetener.
common_user_queries: What is malt syrup used for in Chinese cooking?
Is malt syrup the same as maltose?
What is the glycemic index of malt syrup?
Is malt syrup healthier than sugar?
How many calories in malt syrup?
What is barley malt syrup used for in baking?
Is malt syrup suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F07010003, confidence 0.95), cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central entry for malt syrup. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. All values are consistent across sources.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database and USDA FoodData Central for malt syrup — malt syrup is a carbohydrate-dominant processed cereal extract with no fat by formulation; confirmed zero. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero by the same sources. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived product. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — all fibre is removed during the mashing and filtration process. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available in HPB or USDA sources consulted): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for malt syrup are not fully reported in the HPB Foodo database; USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169652) provides partial micronutrient data but not all fields are available for this processed syrup form. The primary_cuisine tag of 'chinese' from the input record is retained; malt syrup is most prominently used in Chinese culinary tradition as a glaze and confectionery ingredient. Allergen note: malt syrup is derived from barley and contains gluten; it is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance. FoodEx2 code A0CKN is applied as the closest defensible match for malt extract/malt syrup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
